test_text.title: Planning a Trip to a Summer Festival

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a conversation between two friends called George and Nina about a summer music festival.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 and 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, George.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Glad you're back.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Loads of people have phoned you.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I felt just like your secretary.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Sorry.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I went into the library this afternoon to have a look at a newspaper, and I came across something really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: What?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: A book?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: No, a brochure from a summer festival, mainly Spanish music.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Look, I've got it here.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: George says that he found a brochure from a festival, so b has been circled as the answer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 and 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, George.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Glad you're back.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Loads of people have phoned you.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I felt just like your secretary.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Sorry.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I went into the library this afternoon to have a look at a newspaper, and I came across something really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: What?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: A book?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: No, a brochure from a summer festival, mainly Spanish music.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Look, I've got it here.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Spanish music.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: I really love the guitar.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Let's have a look.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: So what's this group 'Guitarrini'?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: They're really good.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: They had a video with all the highlights of the festival at a stand in the lobby to the library.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: So I heard them.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: They play fantastic instruments, drums and flutes and old kinds of guitars.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I've never heard anything like it before.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Sounds great.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: OK, shall we go then?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Spoil ourselves?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Yes, let's.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: The only problem is there aren't any cheap seats. It's all one price.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Well, in that case, we could sit right at the front.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: We'd have a really good view.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Yeah, though, I think that if you sit at the back, you can actually hear the whole thing better.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yes, anyway we can decide when we get there.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 3 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 3 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So will you fill in the form or shall I?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: I'll do it.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Name: George O'Neill.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Address: 48 North Avenue, Westsea.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Do you remember our new postcode?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Still can't remember it.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Just a minute, I've got it written down here.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: WS62YH.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Do you need the phone too?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Please, I'm really bad at numbers.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: So, let's book two tickets for Guitarrini.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: OK, if you're sure £7.50 each is all right.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: How do you feel about the singer?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Hmm. I haven't quite decided, but I've noticed something on the booking form that might just persuade me.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: What's that then?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Free refreshments!
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Yes, look here.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Sunday, 17th of June. Singer, ticket £6.00 includes drinks in the garden.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Sounds like a bargain to me.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Yes, let's book two tickets for that.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: So what else?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: I'm feeling quite keen now.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: How about the pianist on the 22nd of June?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Anna Ventura? I've just remembered that's my evening class night.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Hmm. That's OK.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: I'll just have to go on my own.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: But we can go to the Spanish dance and guitar concert together, can't we?
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm sure Tom and Kieran would enjoy that too.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Good heavens, £10.50 a ticket.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: I can see we're going to have to go without food for the rest of the week.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: We'll need to book four.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: Uh. Wish we were students.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: Look, children, students and senior citizens get a 50% discount on everything.
audioscript.speaker: NINA

audioscript.text: If only.
audioscript.speaker: GEORGE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: The Dinosaur Museum for School Visits

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the education officer of a Dinosaur Museum giving information to some teachers who are planning a school visit.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, and thank you for asking me to your teachers' meeting to talk about the Dinosaur Museum, and to tell you a bit about what you can do with your students there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, let me give you some of the basic information first.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In regard to opening hours, we're open every day of the week from 9 am to 8 pm, except on Mondays, when we close at 1:30 pm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in fact the only day in the year when we're closed is on the 25th of December.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can book a guided tour for your school group any time that we're open.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you bring a school group to the museum, when you arrive, we ask you to remain with your group in the car park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One or more of the tour guides will welcome you there,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and brief you about what the tour will be about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We do this there because our entrance is quite small, and we really haven't got much room for briefing groups in the exhibition area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as the amount of time you'll need goes, if you bring a school group, you should plan on allowing a minimum of 90 minutes for the visit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This allows 15 minutes to get on and off the coach, 45 minutes for the guided tour, and 30 minutes for after-tour activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you're going to have lunch at the museum, you will of course have to allow more time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are two cafes in the museum with seating for 80 people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you want to eat there, you'll need to reserve some seating, as they can get quite crowded at lunchtime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then outside the museum at the back, there are tables, and students can bring their own lunch and eat it there in the open air.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: When the students come into the museum foyer, we ask them to check in their backpacks with their books, lunch boxes, et cetera, at the cloakroom before they enter the museum proper.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm afraid in the past we have had a few things gone missing after school visits, so this is a strict rule.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Also some of the exhibits are fragile,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and we don't want them to be accidentally knocked.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we do provide school students with handouts with questions and quizzes on them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's so much that students can learn in the museum,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and it's fun for them to have something to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course they'll need to bring something to write with for these.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We do allow students to take photographs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For students who are doing projects, it's useful to make some kind of visual record of what they see, that they can add to their reports.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, they should not bring anything to eat into the museum or drinks of any kind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are also a few things that students can do after the tour.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the theatrette on the ground floor, there are continuous screenings of short documentaries about dinosaurs which they can see at any time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We used to have an activity room with more interactive things like making models of dinosaurs, and drawing and painting pictures, even hunting for dinosaur eggs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But unfortunately the room was damaged in a bad storm recently when water came in the roof, so that's closed at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we do have an IT center,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: where students have access to CD ROMs with a range of dinosaur games.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These games are a lot of fun, but they also teach the students about the lives of dinosaurs, how they found food,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: protected their habitat, survived threats, that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I think that's all I have to tell you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Please feel free to ask any questions if you would like to know anything else about...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: We used to have an activity room with more interactive things like making models of dinosaurs, and drawing and painting pictures, even hunting for dinosaur eggs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But unfortunately the room was damaged in a bad storm recently when water came in the roof, so that's closed at the moment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we do have an IT center,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: where students have access to CD ROMs with a range of dinosaur games.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These games are a lot of fun, but they also teach the students about the lives of dinosaurs, how they found food,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: protected their habitat, survived threats, that kind of thing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And I think that's all I have to tell you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Please feel free to ask any questions if you would like to know anything else about...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

School groups are met by tour guides in the <input>.
The whole visit takes 90 minutes, including <input> minutes for the guided tour.
There are <input> behind the museum where students can have lunch.

Which THREE things can students have with them in the museum?

answer_key.correct_answer: 25 December | 25th December | 25th of December | Christmas Day

test_text.title: Feedback on the Geography Society Field Trip Proposal

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a student called Sandra talking to her tutor about a draft proposal she has written for a competition.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, Sandra.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You wanted to see me to get some feedback on your group's proposal, the one you're submitting for the Geography Society Field Trip competition.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I've had a look through your proposal, and I think it's a really good choice.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: In fact, I only have a few things to say about it, but even in an outline document like this.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You really have to be careful to avoid typos and problems with layout in the proposal, and even in the contents page.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So read it through carefully before submitting it, OK?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Will do.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: And I've made a few notes on the proposal about things which could have been better sequenced.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: As for the writing itself, I've annotated the proposal as and where I thought it could be improved.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Generally speaking, I feel you've often used complex structures and long sentences for the sake of it, and as a consequence, although your paragraphing and inclusion of subheadings help.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It's quite hard to follow your train of thought at times.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, so cut them down a bit, can you?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Yes, and don't forget simple formatting like numbering.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Didn't I use page numbers?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I didn't mean that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Look, you've remembered to include headers and footers, which is good, but listing ideas clearly is important.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Number them or use bullet points, which is even clearer.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then you'll focus the reader on your main points.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I thought your suggestion to go to the Navajo tribal park was a very good idea.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I've always wanted to go there.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: My father was a great fan of cowboy films and the Wild West, so I was subjected to seeing all the epics.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Many of which were shot there.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: As a consequence, it feels very familiar to me, and it's awesome both geographically and visually.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: So it's somewhere I've always wanted to visit.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: The subsequent research I did, and the online photographs made me even keener.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Interesting.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right, let's look at the content of your proposal now.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Did you find it comprehensive enough?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well, yes and no.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You've listed several different topics on your contents page.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But I'm not sure they're all relevant.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: No? well, I thought that from the perspective of a field trip, one thing I needed to focus on was the sandstone plateau and cliffs themselves.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Uh. The way they tower up from the flat landscape is just amazing.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: The fact that the surrounding softer rocks were eroded by wind and rain, leaving these huge outcrops high above the plain.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: It's hardly surprising that tourists flock to see the area.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well, yes, I'd agree with including those points.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And then the fact that it's been home to Native American Navajos, and all the social history that goes with that.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: The hardships they endured trying to save their territory from the invading settlers, their culture is so rich, all those wonderful stories.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well, I agree it's interesting, but it's not immediately relevant to your proposal, Sandra.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So, at this stage, I suggest you focus on other considerations.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I think an indication of what the students on the trip could actually do when they get there should be far more central, so that certainly needs to be included, and to be expanded upon.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And I'd like to see something about the local wildlife and vegetation too, not that I imagine there's much to see.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Presumably the tourist invasion hasn't helped.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, I'll do some work on those two areas as well, uh, but you're right, there's not much apart from some very shallow rooted species.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Although it's cold and snowy there in the winter, the earth is baked so hard in the summer sun that rainwater can't penetrate.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Hmm. So it's a case of flood or drought really.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: So I understand.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now, before we look at everything in more detail, I've got a few factual questions for you.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It would be a good idea to include the answers in your finished proposal.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Because they're missing from your draft.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: So you mention the monoliths and the spires which was good, but what area does the tribal park cover?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Do you know?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: 12,000 hectares, and the plain is at about 5,850 meters above sea level.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Hmm, larger than I expected.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, where's the nearest accommodation?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That's a practical detail that you haven't included.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Have you done any research on that?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, there's nowhere to stay in the park itself.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: But there's an old trading post called Goulding quite near. All kinds of tours start from Goulding, too.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Hmm. What kind of tours?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well the most popular are in 4 wheel drive jeeps, but I wouldn't recommend hiring those.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Uh. I think the best way to appreciate the area would be to hire horses instead.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: And trek around on those.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Biking is not allowed, and it's impossible to drive around the area in private vehicles.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: The tracks are too rough.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: OK, lastly, what else is worth visiting there?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: There are several caves, but I haven't looked into any details.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I'll find out about them.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: OK, good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now what I'd like to know is more about...
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecturer's introduction to a geography module.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, welcome to your introductory geography lecture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll begin with some basics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Firstly, what do we learn by studying geography?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, we learn a great deal about all the processes that have affected and that continue to affect the Earth's surface, but we learn far more than that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because studying geography also informs us about the different kinds of relationships that develop between a particular environment and the people that live there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, we like to think of geography as having two main branches.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's the study of the nature of our planet, its physical features, what it actually looks like.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then there's the study of the ways in which we choose to live, and of the impact of those on our planet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our current use of carbon fuels is a good example of that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there are more specific study areas to consider too, and we'll be looking at each of these in turn throughout the semester.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These include biophysical geography.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By which I mean the study of the natural environment and all its living things.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then there's topography, that looks at the shapes of the land and oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's the study of political geography, and social geography too, of course, which is the study of communities of people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have economic geography.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In which we examine all kinds of resources and their use, agriculture for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Next comes historical geography, the understanding of how people and their environments, and the ways they interact have changed over a period of time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And urban geography, an aspect I'm particularly interested in, which takes as its focus the location of cities, the services that those cities provide, and migration of people to and from such cities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And lastly, we have cartography.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's the art and science of map making, you'll be doing a lot of that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, to summarize before we continue, we now have our key answer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Studying this subject is important, because without geographical knowledge, we would know very little about our surroundings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we wouldn't be able to identify all the problems that relate to them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, by definition, we wouldn't be in an informed position to work out how to solve any of them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, now for some practicalities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What do geographers actually do?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, we collect data to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll be doing a lot of that on your first field trip.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How do we do this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are several means.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We might for example conduct a census, count a population in a given area, perhaps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We also need images of the Earth's surface which we can produce by means of computer generation technology, or with the help of satellite relays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've come a very long way from the early exploration of the world by sailing ships.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When geographers only had pens and paper at their disposal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After we've gathered our information, we must analyze it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need to look for patterns, most commonly those of causes and consequences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This kind of information helps us to predict and resolve problems that could affect the world we live in, but we don't keep all this information confidential.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We then need to publish our findings, so that other people can access it and be informed by it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And one way in which this information can be published is in the form of maps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll all have used one at some stage of your life already.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's consider the benefits of maps from a geographer's perspective.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Maps can be folded and put in a pocket.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And can provide a great store of reference when they're collected into an atlas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can depict the physical features of the entire planet if necessary, or just a small part of it in much greater detail.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there is a drawback.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can't exactly replicate something that is three dimensional like our planet on a flat piece of paper, because paper has only two dimensions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that means there'll always be a certain degree of distortion on a map.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It can't be avoided.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We can also use aerial photographs, pictures taken by cameras at high altitude above the earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are great for showing all kinds of geographical features that are not easy to see from the ground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can easily illustrate areas of diseased trees, or how much traffic is on the roads at a given time, or information about deep seabeds for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, there are landsats.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are satellites that circle the earth and transmit visual information to computers at receiving stations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They circle the earth several times a day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And can provide a mass of information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll all be familiar with the information they give us about the weather for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what we're going to do now is look at a short presentation in which you'll see all these tools.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecturer's introduction to a geography module.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So, welcome to your introductory geography lecture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We'll begin with some basics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Firstly, what do we learn by studying geography?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, we learn a great deal about all the processes that have affected and that continue to affect the Earth's surface, but we learn far more than that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because studying geography also informs us about the different kinds of relationships that develop between a particular environment and the people that live there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, we like to think of geography as having two main branches.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's the study of the nature of our planet, its physical features, what it actually looks like.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then there's the study of the ways in which we choose to live, and of the impact of those on our planet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our current use of carbon fuels is a good example of that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there are more specific study areas to consider too, and we'll be looking at each of these in turn throughout the semester.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These include biophysical geography.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By which I mean the study of the natural environment and all its living things.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then there's topography, that looks at the shapes of the land and oceans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's the study of political geography, and social geography too, of course, which is the study of communities of people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We have economic geography.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In which we examine all kinds of resources and their use, agriculture for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Next comes historical geography, the understanding of how people and their environments, and the ways they interact have changed over a period of time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And urban geography, an aspect I'm particularly interested in, which takes as its focus the location of cities, the services that those cities provide, and migration of people to and from such cities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And lastly, we have cartography.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's the art and science of map making, you'll be doing a lot of that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, to summarize before we continue, we now have our key answer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Studying this subject is important, because without geographical knowledge, we would know very little about our surroundings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we wouldn't be able to identify all the problems that relate to them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, by definition, we wouldn't be in an informed position to work out how to solve any of them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, now for some practicalities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What do geographers actually do?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, we collect data to begin with.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll be doing a lot of that on your first field trip.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: How do we do this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are several means.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We might for example conduct a census, count a population in a given area, perhaps.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We also need images of the Earth's surface which we can produce by means of computer generation technology, or with the help of satellite relays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've come a very long way from the early exploration of the world by sailing ships.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When geographers only had pens and paper at their disposal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After we've gathered our information, we must analyze it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need to look for patterns, most commonly those of causes and consequences.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This kind of information helps us to predict and resolve problems that could affect the world we live in, but we don't keep all this information confidential.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We then need to publish our findings, so that other people can access it and be informed by it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And one way in which this information can be published is in the form of maps.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll all have used one at some stage of your life already.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's consider the benefits of maps from a geographer's perspective.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Maps can be folded and put in a pocket.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And can provide a great store of reference when they're collected into an atlas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can depict the physical features of the entire planet if necessary, or just a small part of it in much greater detail.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there is a drawback.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can't exactly replicate something that is three dimensional like our planet on a flat piece of paper, because paper has only two dimensions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that means there'll always be a certain degree of distortion on a map.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It can't be avoided.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We can also use aerial photographs, pictures taken by cameras at high altitude above the earth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are great for showing all kinds of geographical features that are not easy to see from the ground.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can easily illustrate areas of diseased trees, or how much traffic is on the roads at a given time, or information about deep seabeds for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, there are landsats.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are satellites that circle the earth and transmit visual information to computers at receiving stations.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They circle the earth several times a day.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And can provide a mass of information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll all be familiar with the information they give us about the weather for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what we're going to do now is look at a short presentation in which you'll see all these tools.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
